full_response:
We must inspect every possible pure-strategy outcome and verify whether BOTH players are playing a best-response to the other.  If for every outcome at least one player can improve his own payoff by unilaterally changing strategies, then no pure Nash equilibrium (NE) exists.

Notation  
R = Red Pirate Droberts (row player)  
F = First Mate (column player)

Actions  
R :  Greedy (G)   or  Generous (g)  
F :  Mutiny (M)   or  No-Mutiny (¬M)

Payoff matrix  ( F , R )

                F :  M          ¬M
        --------------------------------
        R : G | (3 , 0) | (0 , 3)
        R : g | (1 , 2) | (2 , 1)

We examine each of the four action profiles:

1. (G , M)  →  payoffs (3 , 0)  
   • R’s payoff is 0.  
     If R unilaterally switches to g while F stays at M, payoff becomes (1 , 2); R’s payoff rises from 0 to 2.  
     ⇒ R has a profitable deviation ⇒ not an NE.

2. (G , ¬M) → payoffs (0 , 3)  
   • F’s payoff is 0.  
     If F deviates from ¬M to M while R stays G, payoff becomes (3 , 0); F’s payoff rises from 0 to 3.  
     ⇒ F has a profitable deviation ⇒ not an NE.

3. (g , M) → payoffs (1 , 2)  
   • F’s payoff is 1.  
     If F deviates to ¬M while R stays g, payoff becomes (2 , 1); F’s payoff rises from 1 to 2.  
     ⇒ F has a profitable deviation ⇒ not an NE.

4. (g , ¬M) → payoffs (2 , 1)  
   • R’s payoff is 1.  
     If R deviates to G while F stays ¬M, payoff becomes (0 , 3); R’s payoff rises from 1 to 3.  
     ⇒ R has a profitable deviation ⇒ not an NE.

Since in every pure action profile at least one player can strictly improve his own payoff by deviating unilaterally, no profile satisfies the definition of a pure Nash equilibrium.

Therefore, the game possesses no pure-strategy Nash equilibrium.

\boxed{\text{No pure Nash equilibrium exists; every pure outcome gives at least one player a profitable deviation.}}

Confidence: 0.83
extracted_answer: \text{No pure Nash equilibrium exists; every pure outcome gives at least one player a profitable deviation.}
